title: Washi tape page borders
subtitle: Using decorative tape for headers, borders, and page dividers
excerpt: Washi tape is a thin, semi-transparent, low-tack decorative tape made from Japanese bark-fibre paper. Applied in strips along page margins, across headers, or as tab markers, it adds a clean, removable line of colour and pattern to journal pages, notebooks, and scrapbook spreads without buckling the paper.
sourceNotes: Washi tape as a journalling and decorative craft material came into widespread use after Marks (MT) tape launched as a consumer product in Japan around 2008. The decorative tape category is now associated with multiple manufacturers; the application techniques described here are synthesised from common journalling and bookbinding practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin, semi-transparent, repositionable tape made from washi (Japanese bark-fibre paper). Available in widths from 5 mm to 50 mm and in patterns ranging from solid colours to printed designs.
A neat corner join formed by cutting two tape strips at 45-degree angles so they meet cleanly at the corner point without overlap or gap.
A strip of washi tape applied half on the page edge and half on the page face, then folded over, to create a visible coloured tab for section navigation.

content.text: is repositionable for a short time after application; mistakes can be peeled back and realigned without tearing the page. This low-tack property makes it more forgiving than any adhesive, and the semi-transparency of the tape lets ruled or dotted lines show through, so borders can be placed over existing printed grids.

For a straight border, place the tape roll at one edge of the line and unroll along a ruler held flat against the page. Keep tension on the tape as you apply it; slack tape will wander or develop wrinkles. Cut at the end of the line with scissors or a craft knife against the ruler rather than tearing; torn ends are often angled or ragged. A header strip across the top of a page works the same way: align the bottom edge of the tape to the line you want to mark and press down from one end to the other.

To frame a section of a page, apply the four sides individually. Apply the long edges first, extending past the corners. Apply the short edges second, over the top of the long-edge strips; this creates a layered overlap at the corner. For a cleaner result, cut a
: apply both strips past the corner and use a craft knife to cut through both layers at 45 degrees. Remove the waste pieces and press the cut edges together. The result is a seamless corner join.

Cut a strip of washi tape long enough to extend 10 to 15 mm beyond the page edge. Apply the strip so that half sits on the page face and half extends off the edge. Fold the extending half over onto the page back, pressing it down firmly. The folded strip becomes a
visible from both sides of the page. For a full tab that is visible when the notebook is closed, extend the tape 5 mm further and trim the tab to a consistent length with scissors after folding.

Because washi tape is semi-transparent, two overlapping strips combine their patterns and colours. A solid-colour tape overlaid at the corner with a patterned tape creates a structured border with a decorative accent. Narrow tapes of 5 mm can be layered into pinstripe effects. When layering, apply the base tape first and allow it to sit flat before adding the layer above; pressing too quickly can shift the lower tape.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems applying washi tape to journal pages.

items.symptom: The tape develops wrinkles or bubbles when applied
items.cause: The tape was applied with slack tension, allowing it to fold back on itself.
items.fix: Keep the tape roll slightly taut relative to the page as you apply it, pressing down firmly from the start point as you move along the line. Repositioning works best in the first few seconds; after a minute the adhesive sets.

items.symptom: The tape tears the page surface when removed for repositioning
items.cause: The tape has been on the page for too long and the adhesive has bonded more firmly, or the paper has a rough or porous surface that anchors the adhesive.
items.fix: Peel at a low angle (parallel to the page surface, not upward). On rougher paper, accept that washi tape may not be fully repositionable and plan placement before pressing down.
